is rewritten. TLDR text is unchanged because the TLDR does not invoke the strengthened/relaxed framing.
original_paragraph: The original rule was one of the strongest rhetorical commitments on the dome site — a binary-knockout rule against which our Part 5 was directly pointed. Its replacement is a considerably weaker rule. "Pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, not already covered by a competing explanation" is not a kill-shot rule; it is a methodology-of-good-evidence rule. Under the old rule, the author committed to let any single confirmed row falsify the globe. Under the new rule, he reserves the right to discount any individual row that does not meet the tighter criteria — and the only rows that stand under that rule are roughly the ones we have already assessed as "Std Model Explains" or "Refuted by Data." The rule was strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test — which is the opposite of what a falsification rule is supposed to do.
replacement_html:
Credit the criterion. Taken purely as an epistemic standard, the new rule is the more careful one. "Genuinely pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, and not already covered by a competing explanation" is close to what a working methodologist would actually write down if asked what makes a single observation count as decisive against a global model. The old rule — any single confirmed row falsifies the globe — was rhetorically powerful but epistemically loose, because it counted "confirmed" without first asking whether the row was prospective, whether it discriminated mechanically between dome and globe, or whether the standard model already covered it. Replacing the loose criterion with the tighter one is, on its face, methodological strengthening, and we should say so plainly.

What the criterion does to this page. Strengthening a criterion is not the same as keeping the page that the criterion was originally written for. Applied honestly to the six rows on docs/killshot.html, the new rule does not preserve a kill-shot list; it removes most of the rows. Sydney–Perth is calibration to a known railway distance, so it is not "mechanically discriminating" in the new sense. The Polaris altitude row is a 0.27° excess inside measurement noise covered by atmospheric refraction, so it is not a row "not already covered by a competing explanation." Under the tighter rule, the rows that survive are roughly the ones our review had already classified as Std Model Explains or Refuted by Data — i.e., the rows we were already arguing should never have been kill-shots in the first place. So the new rule is not a rebuttal of our Part 5; applied honestly, it is a concession in the direction of our Part 5, expressed in a more rigorous vocabulary. The page changes function from "falsification instrument" to "methodology-of-good-evidence note" — an evidence-quality exhibit, not a globe-knockout case. We credit the strengthened standard; we observe that under it, the standard is doing our work.
holes_addressed: SEC-5.0.c1 hole #4 (moderate, framing asymmetry): the original single-sentence framing "strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test" collapsed two distinct critiques into one rhetorical move and read as if we were punishing the author for adopting a more rigorous rule. The replacement separates them: paragraph 1 explicitly credits the new rule as an epistemic improvement; paragraph 2 then shows that, applied to THIS page, the new rule does our work for us by removing exactly the rows we were already arguing should not have been kill-shots.
new_evidence_added: Sydney–Perth as calibration-to-a-known-distance fails the "mechanically discriminating" prong of the new rule (cross-reference to Test 1).
Polaris 0.27° altitude excess is inside refraction-modelling noise so it fails the "not already covered by a competing explanation" prong (cross-reference to Test 2).

anticipated_objections.objection: Defender: you are double-counting — first you say the new rule is better, then you say it makes the page worse for him. That is unfair.
anticipated_objections.response: No: a rule can be a better rule in the abstract and still be the wrong fit for the specific page it was retrofitted onto. The new rule is correctly tighter; the page that previously sold itself on the old, looser rule does not survive the tightening. Both observations are simultaneously true and the second is the methodologically interesting one.

anticipated_objections.objection: Defender: you do not know that Sydney–Perth fails "mechanically discriminating" or that Polaris fails "covered by a competing explanation" until the author rules on each row under the new rule. You are assuming the verdicts.
anticipated_objections.response: We are not assuming the author's rulings; we are applying the rule he wrote. Sydney–Perth is a calibration distance taken from a 1912–1917 railway survey — there is no single observation here that mechanically discriminates one cosmology from another, on any reading. The Polaris excess is 0.27° at high latitude where refraction routinely explains arc-minute-scale offsets — that IS "already covered by a competing explanation" in the standard sense. The author is free to disagree, but until he writes a row-level survival argument under his own new rule, the natural application is the one we describe.

anticipated_objections.objection: Defender: the page still lists Aug 2026 eclipse, NMP drift, SAA field, and JFK–LHR as PENDING under the new rule. So the page is not converted to a methodology-of-good-evidence note — it still has live kill-shot candidates.
anticipated_objections.response: Correct, and we do not deny that. The PENDING rows are the ones whose verdict windows are still open; they are not the rows the 2026-04-17 edit demoted. Our paragraph is about what the new rule does to the rows that the same edit reclassified as STRUCTURAL SUPPORT / SUPPORTIVE ONLY. The PENDING rows are addressed in their own subsections and remain testable on their own merits — Section 5.0 simply notes that the demoted rows fall out cleanly under the strengthened rule.

kernel_of_truth: The author did, on net, raise his epistemic bar with the 2026-04-17 rewrite. "Pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, not already covered by a competing explanation" is a tighter standard than "any single confirmed row falsifies the globe," and a critic who refuses to acknowledge that comes across as nit-picking a good-faith methodological upgrade. The kernel of truth is that this is a genuine improvement and should be credited. What it does not do is rescue the demoted rows: Sydney–Perth and Polaris fail the new criterion just as cleanly as our review found them failing on the merits.
tldr_change_reason: The current ps-tldr does not invoke the strengthened/relaxed framing; it summarises the badge demotions, the post-window pattern, and the CSS-class mismatch. None of those are touched by this patch.
notes_for_decider: Targeted in-place paragraph replacement inside <details id="ks-april-2026-update"> in data/sections.json -> part5.html. The original paragraph is provided verbatim under original_paragraph for exact match; the replacement under replacement_html. No other text in the section is touched. Because this is paragraph-level surgery, please verify the exact original text still matches sections.json at integration time (no other patch should have touched this paragraph between EXP-193 author time and integration time). If it has changed, re-anchor on the substring "strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test" and re-confirm with the human operator before applying. ISS-1217 should close on integration; no related issues are affected.
